Fnrn Polrrrcnr- PRACrrcEs C oMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o gsglnrento, CA g5gt1_232g
(916) 322-5660 e Fax (916) 322_0886
November 22,2010
Jannie L. Quinn
City Attorney
500 Castro Street
Mountain View, CA 94039-7 540
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-10-172
Dear Ms. Quinn:
This letter respgnls to yollr request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r rnii tetteiis based solely on the facts
presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
advice' (In re oglesby (1975) I FPPC ops. 71-) Because you do not seek advice on a specific
governmental decision, we can provide you only with informal assistance.2 wotrring in this letter
may be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place.
QUESTTON
will City Councilmember John Inks be able to participate in governmental decisions
regarding high speed rail ("HSR") in his area despit" uiirquolifying-conflict of interest based on
the "public generally exception,,?
CONCLUSION
Based on your facts, the requirements of the "public generally" exception appear to be
met, allowing Councilmember Inks to participate in g^overnmental decisions regarding the HSR.
I The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections g1000 through gl0l4. All statutory
ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8lt0 through l8gg7 of ritle 2 of the Calitbrnia code of Regulations. All
regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of thi Califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
r Informal assistance does not offer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or fbrmal written
advice. (Regulation t8329(c)(3).)


File No. l-10-172
Page No. 2
You are the city attorney for the City of Mountain View and you represent City
Councilmember John Inks. Mountain view is one of several cities involved in discussions
regarding High Speed Rail and how it might fit within and impact their cities. Mountain View is
bisected by a train corridor, currentlyt.,r"d by CalTrain for commuter train runs and by Soilhem
Pacific Railroad for off-hour freight service. Throughout Mountain view, there are two sets of
tracks that run approximately 500 passenger trains pir week. The trains are pushed or pulled by
diesel engines with their associated.ounds and exhaust.
Generallr,.lhg train lines' operators control the frequency, number, and length of trains
and the city council does not have input. The HSR Authority *itt pt* and construct a rail
system between San Francisco and Los Angeles. Mountain View *itt U" included as a stop
along this rail system. unlike the traditionil train systems, however, the city council will have
limited input.
The HSR trains will be electric, rather than diesel, which will be quieter and cleaner than
the current trains- Eventually, the HSR Authority envisions up to two new sets of tracks to be
laid in the corridor, but might use existing tracks until then. The plan for HSR is not set and is
likely to undergo several updates before implementation.
The HSR Authority will make the decisions regarding this project, but the city council
will be asked for input regarding the draft Environmen-tal Impact n.port and potential iterations
9f HSR' including elevating or undergrounding the tracks orih" placement oi a mid-peninsula
HSR station within the city.
Councilmember Inks lives in a condominium unit in Mountain View, which he owns.
The complex abuts the train corridor and Mr. Ink's unit is within the 500 feet of the corridor.
Additionally, over l07o of Mountain view's residential properties are also within 500 feet of the
train corridor. The HSR plans do not contemplat" *y p't yrical change to the corridor within 500
feet of Councilmemter Ink's complex, but may "omid., 
a station further south. It might also
consider elevating the tracks within the conidor near his ownership interest.
The city council will encounter decisions related to whether the HSR track should be
below grade, at grade, or elevated, where to locate the mid-peninsula station, and whether the
City of Mountain view should contribute financially to the HSR project.
You have stated that Councilmember Inks has a conflict of interest based on his
ownership of his condo and its proximity to the train corridor.
FACTS
AIIALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, parricipating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "fi-nancial interest,, in a governmental


File No. I-rc-nz
Page No. 3
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect on one or more of the public oifi.iol', economic interests. (Section
87 103 ; Regulation I 8700(a).)
while the commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interist in a given governmental decision, we do not
engage in the full analysis here. You have explained thalcouncilmember Inks is a public
official' who will be-making a governmental decision that is reasonabty ro."s"Loble to have a
material financial effect on his economic interest. For this reason, we will assume the conflict,
by virtue of the councilmember's property ownership interest, and analyze steps seven and eight.
You have suggested that the analysis might be different depending on the stage of the
q:ojecl.Td the type of decision. Based on reaairg your facts, Councilmember Inks,
disqualifying conflict of interest applies to all aspJcis of the project. Even so, we enclose
Regulation 18709 for your review, which addresses segmenting decisions, should you need to
apply the same principals in determining whether u"y J*"Lption applies.
we assume there is a disqualifying conflict of interest on all decisions regarding the HSR
and apply Steps seven and Eight of thl analysis to determine if any exception applies.
Step Seven: Does the,,public Generally, Exception Apply?
Even if an official has a conflict of 
^interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the *unt eii, which the decision will affect the public generally.
(section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) Regulation 18707(b) sets forth u rour-Jt"p process to
determine "if the effect of a decision is not distinguishabl" f.o.., the effect on the public
generally:"
"(l) Step one: Identify each specific person or real property (economic
interest) that is materially affected bythe gor"--"ntar decision.
"(2) Step Two: For each person or real property identified in Step one,
determine the_appricable 'significant segment, rure according to the
provisions of [Regulation I g707. 1 (b)].
"(3) Step Three: Determine if the significant segment is affected by the
governmental decision as set forth in the applicable 'significant selment,
rule. If the answer is 'no,' then the analysii'ends because the firstf,rong
of a two-part test set forth in [Regulation tgzoz.t(b)] is not met, and the
public official cannot participate in the govern*"r,oi decision. If the
answer is 'yes,' proceed to Step Four.
"(4) Step Four: Following the provisions of [Regulation r g707.1(bx2)],
determine if the person or real property identified in Step one is affected


File No. I-rc-172
Page No.4
by the governmental decision in 'substantially the same manner, as other
persons or real property in the applicable significant segment. If the
answer is 'yes' a-s to each pe.sonb. re.al pro-perty identiTied in ste; one,
then the effect of the decision is not aistingJisnoui" r.orr1 the effect on the
public generally and the public official mfr participare in the decision. If
the answer i:. io. as to any per:9n or rear irtperty-identified in step one,
the public officja-l may not pirticipate in the gou.**rrrtal decision unless
one of the special rures set forth in [RegulatiJn s ft707.zthrough rclw.g]
applies to each person or real property triggering the conflict of interest.,,
(Regulation I 8707(bX I )_(4).)
Step One:
Real Property -- As discussed above, you have indicated a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on councilmember Irk', ."onomic interest in real property that is 500
feet or less from the train corridor.
Step Two:
Identifv significant segment. Regu^lation 18707.1(b)(rXB) explains how to determine
whether the govemmental aecision will affect a signiticani segment of the relevant jurisdiction
when an official's real property is involved.
"(B) For decisionsthat affect a public official's interest in real property,
the decision also affects:
"(i) l0 percent or more of all property owners or all residential
property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district
the official represents; or
"(ii) 5,000 property owners in the jurisdiction of the officials,
agency."
Step Three:
You have determined, through GPS mapping of the train corridor that at least l0 percent
of Mountain View's property owners are within 500 feet of the train corridor. Given the
assumption that those properties that are within this distance from the train corridor will be
affected, the significant segment prong of the test is met.3
Step Four:
s
The final step requires councilmember Inks to determine the financial effects on his
respective economic interests and measure these effects oguirr, the financial effects on the public
generally' The inquiry must focus on indentifying wtreitre?he minimum threshold of the
significant segment identified is affected in ,uLrta'ntiuily tt " 
same manner.
f'eet.
' For purposes of determining the significant segment, you are not limited to these properties within 500


File No. l-rc-I7}
Page No. 5
- 
Many factors play a role in determining whether or not economic interests will be
financially affected in a manner substantially the same as the identified significant segment
including, but not limited to: the proximity of a property or business to a specific project or
property subject to the govemment decision; location oi a property or business; impact on an
individual, property, or business from noise or traffic; impact on a business or property due to
{9v9!orment potential; and the size of a property or business. (See BergerAdvice Letter, No. A-
05-054; also see Regurlation 18707.1(b)filfal ("opy enclosed).)
As we are not the finder of facts, we cannot conclusively determine whether Step Four of
this test is met and you have not provided information on this point. we can say, however, that
the relevant factors in Regulation 18707.1(b)(2xA) do suggesithat if the HSR is using the tracks
throughout Mountain view in the same way, it does seem-that all residential properties that are
within 500 feet of the corridor would be similarly affected. we leave this deLrmination to you
and Councilmember Inks, however. a
Councilmember Inks must make this determination on a decision-by-decision basis, in
the even that some decisions in the course of these proceedings affect rri, prop.rty in a different
manner than the significant segment.
step Eight. Is councilmember rnk's participation ..rega[y required,,?
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualiii"a ir the officiar is legally required to participate.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18708.) You irave not presented any facts indicating that this
exception applies here.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
NI,ll" M{l^^.u-
By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
HMRjgl
o It ul example, forreal property economic interests, we have previously advised that the financial effect
on properties within the "significant segment" could vary uy ,'rp."iti"a financial range equal to plus or minus two-
percent of the value of the public official's property and stiil be tonsidered to be aff'eJted in ..substantially the same
manner" as the official's property. For example, if the public orn.irt;r property was valued at $500,000, and the
t'inancial effect of the decision is a three-percent inc.eore ($ 15,000) ;;the offlcial,s property, any increases between
$5'000 and $25'000 on p-ropgrtils withinihe signiticant ,"g*.nt *ouio u" considerei ,,iustanriarty rhe same.
(Berger Advice Letter, No. 4-05-054.)
